bibliographic_data.title: The complexity of approximating the matching polynomial in the complex plane
bibliographic_data.abstract: We study the problem of approximating the value of the matching polynomial on graphs with edge parameter γ, where γ takes arbitrary values in the complex plane. When γ is a positive real, Jerrum and Sinclair showed that the problem admits an FPRAS on general graphs. For general complex values of γ, Patel and Regts, building on methods developed by Barvinok, showed that the problem admits an FPTAS on graphs of maximum degree ∆ as long as γ is not a negative real number less than or equal to −1/(4(∆−1)). Our first main result completes the picture for the approximability of the matching polynomial on bounded degree graphs. We show that for all ∆≥3 and all real γ less than −1/(4(∆−1)), the problem of approximating the value of the matching polynomial on graphs of maximum degree ∆ with edge parameter γ is #P-hard.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper shows that for all ∆≥3 and all real γ less than −1/(4(∆−1)), the problem of approximating the value of the matching polynomial on graphs of maximum degree ∆ with edge parameter γ is #P-hard.
core_content.key_methodology: The authors use correlation decay techniques and complexity-theoretic arguments to prove that the problem is #P-hard under certain conditions.
outcomes: For all ∆≥3 and all real γ less than −1/(4(∆−1)), it is #P-hard to approximate ZG(γ) on graphs with degree at most ∆.
It is #P-hard to approximate |ZG(γ)| within a factor of 1.01 on bipartite graphs of maximum degree ∆, even when restricted to bipartite graphs G with ZG(γ) ≠ 0.
It is #P-hard to compute the sign of ZG(γ) on bipartite graphs of maximum degree ∆.
core_content.full_text_markdown: The paper investigates the complexity of approximating the matching polynomial in the complex plane. For positive real γ, Jerrum and Sinclair showed that an FPRAS exists for general graphs. Patel and Regts extended this to a more general case where γ is not a negative real number less than or equal to −1/(4(∆−1)). The authors complete this picture by proving that for all ∆≥3 and all real γ < -1/(4(∆-1)), the problem of approximating ZG(γ) on graphs with maximum degree ∆ is #P-hard. They also show that it is #P-hard to approximate |ZG(γ)| within a factor of 1.01 on bipartite graphs and that it is #P-hard to compute the sign of ZG(γ).
